nativeRange.habitat: Scattered oceanic islands across the Indo-Pacific; tends to inhabit clear waters along steep drop-offs at depths of roughly 10–120 m, more commonly 25–120 m, frequenting caves, overhangs and crevices and rarely observed in shallower than 30 m

diet.notes: Generally accepts a range of large meaty marine foods including krill, raw shrimp, chopped marine fish, squid, clam and similar items. Frozen mysis or enriched brine, fiddler crabs and other small meaty live foods have been reported to be readily accepted. Feeder fish are best avoided as a primary food source; feeding every other day to a few times per week is typically recommended for adults.

goodWith: robust predatory tankmates of comparable size

summary: A strikingly colored deep-reef grouper from scattered Indo-Pacific islands; generally considered an aggressive ambush predator best suited to large fish-only displays alongside robust tankmates.
careNotes:
The Pollenei Grouper (Cephalopholis polleni), also widely sold as the Harlequin Hind or Polleni Grouper, is a medium-large reef-associated grouper distributed across scattered oceanic islands of the Indo-Pacific (Comoros and Mauritius east through the Maldives to the Line Islands and French Polynesia), generally absent from continental Indian Ocean coasts, Australia, New Guinea and the major Indonesian islands. In the wild it tends to occur along clear-water drop-offs at moderate-to-deep reef depths and is commonly associated with caves and overhangs.

It is generally considered intermediate to keep, with the primary challenges being its eventual size, heavy meaty waste output and pronounced predatory behavior. Maximum size reports vary across sources: LiveAquaria lists about 14 in (≈36 cm), Quality Marine lists about 16 in (≈41 cm), and FishBase gives a maximum total length of 43 cm — figures that generally align on a medium-large adult of roughly 35–43 cm. It has been documented to behave as a solitary ambush predator (trophic level ~4.2) that consumes fishes and crustaceans, and it will readily eat any tankmate it can fit in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp and small fish.

Minimum tank size shows moderate disagreement between hobby sources (LiveAquaria recommends 250 gallons / ~946 L, Quality Marine recommends 150 gallons / ~568 L). The value used here (1000 L / ~265 gal) intentionally biases toward the larger figure to provide adequate swimming volume, footprint and waste capacity for a fully grown adult predator. A long tank with substantial live rock structure for caves and overhangs and ample open swimming space along the front is generally advised.

Like most groupers in Cephalopholis, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite; captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. It has no venomous spines and is not considered a notable jumper, though a secure lid is generally sensible for any large active predator. Reef compatibility is commonly listed as 'with caution': adult specimens pose minimal direct threat to corals but will readily consume mobile reef invertebrates and small fish.

The modern accepted family is Epinephelidae (per FishBase); older hobby references and some retailers still list this species under Serranidae, which historically subsumed the groupers.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Cephalopholis polleni has not been documented in hobby literature. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish may transition to male as it grows; specific spawning behavior in the wild is poorly characterized.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: 250 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025, care level moderate, temperament aggressive, reef safe with caution, max size 14 in (~36 cm), family listed as Serranidae (older classification).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae / Perciformes), maximum size (43 cm TL), depth and temperature ranges (20.7–28.2°C, mean 26.5°C), and global distribution. FishBase wild temperature mean is slightly higher than aquarium-hobby ranges; reported as Least Concern (IUCN 2018).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (16 in / ~41 cm), minimum tank size (150 gal / ~568 L — notably smaller than LiveAquaria's 250 gal), care level (listed easy — averaged with LiveAquaria 'moderate' to give 'intermediate'), water parameters (matched LiveAquaria), temperament (aggressive), diet and feeding-frequency guidance. Tank-size disagreement biased toward the larger figure per playbook.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. In the wild, as a protogynous hermaphrodite, dominant females may transition to male as they grow.
